data_in.description: Backed AnnData object with fragment data in .obsm

data_out.description: Count matrix with tile coordinates and paired-insertion counts
Matrix dimensions (n_cells × n_tiles) and sparsity statistics

domain_knowledge: Paired-insertion counting quantifies chromatin accessibility by counting DNA fragment insertions at both ends, differing from insertion-based counting which counts single insertion sites and fragment-based counting which counts whole fragments.
Tile-based matrices partition the genome into fixed-size intervals (typically 5 kb) for efficient large-scale analysis, enabling scalability to millions of cells.

Backed AnnData objects store data on disk rather than in memory, enabling analysis of datasets larger than available RAM; fragment data is stored in .obsm['fragment_paired'] for paired-end data or .obsm['fragment_single'] for single-end data.

The counting_strategy parameter in pp.add_tile_matrix was introduced in Release 2.6.0 to allow flexible choice between insertion-based, fragment-based, and paired-insertion strategies for feature quantification.

direct_checks: verify file exists: AnnData object with .uns['fragment_file'] or .obm containing fragment data
script_runs: pp.add_tile_matrix(adata, counting_strategy='paired_insertion') executes without error on the input AnnData object
file_format_is: resulting count matrix is stored in adata.X or adata.obm as sparse matrix or dense array
row_count_equals: count matrix has number of rows equal to number of observations (cells) in input AnnData
field_present: verify 'tile_matrix' or equivalent matrix key exists in adata.obm after function call
value_in_range: count matrix contains at least one non-zero entry (sparsity < 100%), robust to different tile sizes and fragment distributions
output_matches_reference: if reference output matrix exists in repository test data or documentation, byte-for-byte comparison for exact parameter set
expert_review: verify that paired_insertion counting logic correctly counts both read1 and read2 insertions from paired-end fragments, not total fragment count
assess whether tile matrix dimensions (number of tiles × cells) are biologically reasonable for the input fragment file size and genome coverage
confirm that non-zero entries in count matrix correspond to tiles with actual fragment coverage and do not represent computational artifacts

cmd: import snapatac2 as snap; import anndata as ad; adata = ad.read_h5ad('{inputs.adata_backed}', backed='r'); snap.pp.add_tile_matrix(adata, paired_insertion=True); print(f'Tile matrix shape: {adata.obsm["tile_matrix"].shape}'); print(f'Non-zero entries: {adata.obsm["tile_matrix"].nnz}'); print(f'Sparsity: {1 - adata.obsm["tile_matrix"].nnz / (adata.obsm["tile_matrix"].shape[0] * adata.obsm["tile_matrix"].shape[1])}')

Synthesis grounding: the following tools/outputs were NOT found in the source paper and are inferred — verify before use: Count matrix with tile coordinates and paired-insertion counts, Matrix dimensions (n_cells × n_tiles) and sparsity statistics.
workflow_description: 1. Load backed AnnData object containing fragment data (stored in .obsm['fragment_paired'] or .obsm['fragment_single']). 2. Invoke pp.add_tile_matrix with counting_strategy='paired_insertion' to quantify chromatin accessibility at fixed genomic intervals. 3. Extract the resulting count matrix and verify matrix shape (n_obs × n_vars) matches tile coordinates. 4. Validate that count matrix contains non-zero entries and that paired-insertion counts are distributed across cells and genomic tiles as expected.
